name: Groen, Eric L.
recent_1: Vlaskamp, D., Pollitt, A., Blundell, J., Landman, A., Groen, E. L., van Paassen, M. M., Stroosma, O., & Mulder, M. (2026). Cabin crew startle and surprise: occurrence and impact. Cognition, Technology & Work. DOI: 10.1007/s10111-026-00866-5
recent_1_synopsis: This study investigates the prevalence and operational impact of startle and surprise events among cabin crew in aviation contexts. It extends prior work on flight-deck startle (piloting) to cabin operations, examining how unexpected events affect crew response and performance. The findings provide empirical data to support startle management training across the full aviation crew complement.
recent_2: Landman, A., Stuldreher, I. V., Van der Burg, E., Evertsen, F. W., Reuten, A. J. C., Ledegang, W. D., Houben, M. M. J., & Groen, E. L. (2025). Pilots gaze more outside while performing an auditory cognitive task. Ergonomics, 69(5), 881-891. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2025.2500526
recent_2_synopsis: Using two simulator experiments (one fixed-base with novice military pilots and one moving-base with experienced helicopter pilots), this study examines how adding an auditory cognitive task shifts pilot gaze behavior during manual flight. Results show pilots direct more gaze outside the cockpit under dual-task load, with implications for cockpit design and pilot workload management. The work contributes to understanding attention allocation and task interference in aviation.
recent_3: Vlaskamp, D., Pollitt, A., Blundell, J., Landman, A., Groen, E. L., van Paassen, M. M., Stroosma, O., & Mulder, M. (2025). Startle and surprise in helicopter operations: reported prevalence and application of mitigation strategies. Cognition, Technology & Work, 27(3), 579-590. DOI: 10.1007/s10111-025-00811-y
recent_3_synopsis: This paper surveys helicopter pilots to characterize the prevalence of startle and surprise events during rotary-wing operations and how crews apply mitigation strategies. It adapts a conceptual framework of startle and surprise (developed for fixed-wing aviation) to the specific operational challenges of helicopters, including low-altitude and confined-area flight. Findings inform training recommendations tailored to helicopter crews.
seminal_1: Bles, W., Bos, J. E., de Graaf, B., Groen, E., & Wertheim, A. H. (1998). Motion sickness: only one provocative conflict? Brain Research Bulletin, 47(5), 481-487. DOI: 10.1016/S0361-9230(98)00115-4
seminal_1_synopsis: This foundational paper (approximately 494 citations as of June 2026 per Google Scholar) proposes a unifying framework for motion sickness: a single type of sensory conflict -- between the expected and the actual sensory input derived from an internal model of the body's orientation -- is necessary and sufficient to explain all forms of motion sickness. By formalizing the 'subjective vertical conflict' hypothesis, it replaced more diffuse sensory rearrangement theories and became the dominant explanatory model in the field. The mathematical formulation provided the basis for predictive models of motion sickness in simulation, automotive, and maritime contexts.
seminal_2: Bos, J. E., Bles, W., & Groen, E. L. (2008). A theory on visually induced motion sickness. Displays, 29(2), 47-57. DOI: 10.1016/j.displa.2007.09.002
seminal_2_synopsis: Cited approximately 416 times (Google Scholar, June 2026), this paper extends the 1998 conflict model to explain visually induced (i.e., non-physical) motion sickness -- a phenomenon increasingly relevant with the proliferation of display technologies, virtual reality, and vehicle HMIs. The theory describes how visual motion information interacts with the vestibular system to generate a subjective vertical conflict that produces nausea and discomfort even without physical motion. Its mechanistic, predictive account has been highly influential for display system design, simulation validation, and autonomous vehicle interior ergonomics.
expertise_overview: Eric L. Groen's research centers on human spatial orientation, vestibular physiology, and their applied consequences in aviation and simulator environments. He investigates how pilots perceive and misperceive their own motion and attitude -- particularly under spatial disorientation, the vestibular Coriolis illusion, the 'leans' illusion, and otolith-mediated tilt-translation ambiguity -- using motion-base simulators and psychophysical paradigms. A major strand of his work examines the cognitive and behavioral sequelae of spatial disorientation, including interference with pilot decision-making, attention, and workload during flight. He has made seminal contributions to the theory and modeling of motion sickness (both physical and visually induced), formalizing the subjective vertical conflict model that now underpins predictive algorithms used in transportation and display design. His applied work includes development of the TNO Spatial Disorientation Investigation Tool for accident analysis, galvanic vestibular stimulation as a low-cost illusion induction method in fixed-base simulators, and pilot training for unexpected events including startle, surprise, and upset recovery. More recently his research group has expanded into physiological monitoring (gaze behavior, brain-computer interfaces, hypoxia detection) and extended startle-and-surprise research from fixed-wing cockpits to helicopter and cabin-crew contexts.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Sources: (1) ResearchGate profile (https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Eric-Groen-2) listing TNO (Delft) affiliation and Cranfield University visiting professorship; (2) Google Scholar profile (https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=DsySLQMAAAAJ) showing 3,435 total citations and h-index 28 as of June 2026; (3) TNO publications repository; (4) six pre-verified Human Factors DOIs provided as ground-truth anchor. Citation counts for seminal papers sourced from Google Scholar, June 2026. All five DOIs in this record verified via Crossref API (HTTP 200, matching titles and authors): 10.1016/S0361-9230(98)00115-4 (Bles et al. 1998), 10.1016/j.displa.2007.09.002 (Bos et al. 2008), 10.1007/s10111-026-00866-5 (Vlaskamp et al. 2026), 10.1080/00140139.2025.2500526 (Landman et al. 2025), 10.1007/s10111-025-00811-y (Vlaskamp et al. 2025). No unverifiable citations.
